title: Electronic and Vibrational Properties of On-Surface Synthesized Gulf-Edged Chiral Graphene Nanoribbons
authors: Xuanchen Li, Amogh Kinikar, Vikas Sharma, Andres Ortega Guerrero, George F. S. Whitehead, Mickael Lucien Perrin, Carlo A. Pignedoli, Roman Fasel, Ashok Keerthi, Gabriela Borin Barin
abstract: On-surface synthesis enables the fabrication of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) with atomic precision, allowing their electronic, optical, and magnetic properties to be tuned by engineering edge structure and width. Progress on the synthesis of chiral GNRs has nevertheless remained limited, largely because existing precursor designs rely on laterally fused acene units and cannot access edge topologies beyond armchair and zigzag. Here, we introduce a new on-surface synthesis motif that yields a gulf-edged chiral GNR. The growth steps are monitored by scanning probe microscopy, and the atomic structure is confirmed by non-contact atomic force microscopy. Scanning tunneling spectroscopy combined with theoretical simulations identifies the gulf-edged chiral GNR as a closed-shell semiconductor with a bandgap of 1.8 eV. Raman spectroscopy reveals vibrational properties, including a distinctive mode that may serve as a fingerprint for chiral GNRs. The Raman analysis further uncovers ambient instability despite the large bandgap and non-spin-polarized edges, consistent with prior reports linking GNR stability to zigzag edge features. This work establishes a rationally designed synthesis motif for chiral GNRs and provides a combined structural, electronic, and vibrational characterization, offering guidelines for future synthesis strategies.
summary: This paper reports the first successful synthesis of graphene nanoribbons with a unique 'gulf-edged' chiral topology. By using a new on-surface synthesis motif, the researchers created atomically precise ribbons that act as closed-shell semiconductors. The study identifies specific Raman modes as structural fingerprints and reveals that zigzag edge segments cause the ribbons to be unstable in ambient air. This work provides a new strategy for engineering complex edge structures in carbon-based nanostructures.
main_problem: The difficulty in synthesizing chiral graphene nanoribbons (chGNRs) with complex edge topologies, such as 'gulf' or 'cove' edges, beyond standard armchair and zigzag structures.
main_result: The successful on-surface synthesis of a new class of gulf-edged chiral GNRs, characterized as closed-shell semiconductors with a 1.8 eV bandgap and specific Raman vibrational fingerprints.
method: A combination of bottom-up organic synthesis, scanning probe microscopy (STM, nc-AFM), scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS), Raman spectroscopy, and Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations.
model_or_system: The physical system is a (4,2,7)-chiral graphene nanoribbon (chGNR) with a gulf-edged topology, synthesized via surface-assisted polymerization and cyclodehydrogenation of a molecular precursor on an Au(111) surface.
key_observables: Electronic bandgap (1.8 eV), Raman vibrational modes (CH/D and G bands), and atomic-scale structural topography.
important_parameters: Bandgap of 1.8 eV, thermal stability up to 673 K, and ambient instability due to zigzag edge segments.
main_assumptions: The use of periodic DFT to approximate the electronic and vibrational properties of the extended nanoribbon structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the synthetic route and final structure; Figure 2 displays STM and nc-AFM images of the synthesis progression; Figure 3 presents electronic characterization; Figure 4 illustrates Raman spectra degradation upon air exposure.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the design and chemical synthesis of precursors, followed by the on-surface synthesis process and structural confirmation via microscopy, then moves to electronic and vibrational characterization, and concludes with an analysis of ambient stability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Synthetic route towards the (4,2,7)-chGNR. Reagents and conditions: (a) 4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-2- (naphthalen-1-yl)-1,3,2-dioxaborolane, K2CO3, Pd2(dba)3, SPhos, toluene, water, ethanol, 383 K , 18h, 80%. (b) BBr3, DCM, 273 K to 295 K , 18h, 99%. (c) trifluoromethanesulfonic anhydride, pyridine, DCM, rt, 12h, 72%. (d) Compound 1c, K3PO4, dry toluene, SPhos, Pd(dppf)Cl2, 373 K , 24h, 85% (details on 1c are provided in the Supporting Information). (e) ICl, DCM, 203 K, 2h, 93%. (f) Sublimation onto Au(111) , then annealing at 438 K for 30 min. (g) Annealing at 673 K for 30 min. The structure of (4,2,7)-chGNR is shown and labeled as 7. The chiral vector Ch(n, m) and the width w are...

Figure 2: On-surface synthesis of (4,2,7)-chGNR via surface-assisted dehalogenation and polymerization of monomer 5, followed by cyclodehydrogenation of polymer 6. (a–c) STM topography image of monomers 5, polymer 6, and (4,2,7)-chGNR 7 on the Au(111) surface. All STM measurements were performed at 4.5 K. Scanning parameters: (a) −0.4 V, 20 pA; (b) 1 V, 50 pA; (c) −1 V, 20 pA. The STM images were post-processed by subtracting a plane fitted through three reference points. (d) High-resolution nc-AFM frequency shift image of (4,2,7)-chGNR using a CO-functionalized tip with oscillation amplitude of 100 pm. The nc-AFM measurement was also performed at 4.5 K.

Figure 3: Electronic characterization of the (4,2,7)-chGNR. (a,b) The STS measurements in (b) were ac- quired on the 5-unit (4,2,7)-chGNR segment shown in (a). The spectra are color-coded to their STS positions in (a). A CO-functionalized tip was used to acquire STS spectra. (c) Band structure (energy versus wave vector k) (left, black; a is the lattice parameter) and density of states (right, grey) calculated for an infinitely long (4,2,7)-chGNR with Kohn–Sham DFT. Valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) are highlighted. (d, f) Constant current differential conductance (dI/dV) maps acquired over a 5-unit (4,2,7)-chGNR seg- ment at the energy of the valence band and conduction band. STS...

Figure 4: High-frequency region of the Raman spectrum for (4,2,7)-chGNR and the DFT-simulated vibra- tional modes. (a) Experimental Raman spectra of pristine and air-exposed (4,2,7)-chGNR under 532 nm excitation, along with the DFT-simulated Raman spectrum. The simulated spectrum is obtained by sum- ming Lorentzian peaks with a broadening of 9 cm−1 at the calculated vibrational frequencies. In the exper- imental spectrum of pristine (4,2,7)-chGNR, the D and G peak regions are deconvoluted using Lorentzian fits. Vertical lines beneath the spectra show a comparison between experimentally determined Raman peak positions obtained through deconvolution, and those obtained from DFT simulation....
